# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I crave romance and tenderness

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I mean, my title says it all. I want a movie that has straight-up mushy romance. I'm going through a rejection, and I want absolutely the mushiest, sweetest, tender, most unrealistic love story ever. I don't even want a rom-com, because I just want the "rom" part. I want something that reminds me of Surrender by Su\*cide (censoring so I don't have to make this post 18+), or Heaven by Bryan Adams. Please, I'm desperate for yearning !!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Somewhere In Time
2. I’m surprised no one has said Pride & Prejudice (2005) yet. 
3. Sorry, I read the post title before knowing which community I was in 🫣
4. Roman Holiday (1953)
5. Garden State (2004)
6. In Time   ❌️

**ABOUT TIME** ✅️
7. I forgot Amelie!
8. Before Sunrise trilogy is exactly what you need, just two people falling in love over a night and then nine years of longing for each other, pure tenderness the whole way through.
9. Untamed Heart!!! One of my all-time favorite comfort movies. Don't watch the trailer--it gives too much away. You can watch the movie for free on YouTube right now.
10. Sense and sensibility

## 2. Need some lighthearted, feel-good comedies to turn my brain off.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** To give you an idea of my taste, here are some comedy movies that I absolutely love and have watched multiple times:

* Get Him to the Greek
* Role Models
* Waiting...
* Knocked Up
* Happy Gilmore
* 50 First Dates
* We're the Millers
* Borat
* Crazy, Stupid, Love.
* Good Fortune
* The Dictator
* Accepted
* Pineapple Express
* The Interview
* The 40-Year-Old Virgin
* The Disaster Artist
* Forgetting Sarah Marshall
* I Love You, Man
* The Promotion
* 21 Jump Street
* 22 Jump Street
* The Jerk

Looking forward to your recommendations! Thanks in advance!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Game Night
  - "How can that be profitable for Frito Lays?"
2. Consider the Mel Brooks classics:  Young Frankenstein, Men in Tights, Blazing Saddles.
  - Also High Anxiety and Spaceballs
3. Snatch (2000) and if u like that u will also like the hot fuzz (2007)
4. Hot rod
5. The Three Amigos

Super Troopers

Meatballs
6. Blast from the past!

Wedding crashers 

Tropic thunder

Not another teen movie

You’re cordially invited 

Sweethearts
7. Raising Arizona
A Fish Called Wanda

8. Hot tub Time Machine 

## 3. Coming of Age Movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Any movie recommendations, specially about young males, that are coming of age? Boys that deal with bullying, having trouble fitting in, maybe they don't have great relationships with their parents, even movies that highlight relationships with big/little brothers or even father/sons. I really enjoy dynamics with big brothers protecting and looking out for their little brothers, especially if he doesnt fit in at school. No, that don't mean they gotta get along and never fight or argue, haha! Brothers/fathers and sons argue sometimes (obviously)

A few movies I'll use as examples: Jet Boy (2001), Honey Boy (2019), Local Boys (2002), and even The Black Phone (2021), (yes I know that one's a horror movie, but I love how it still shows the troubles of young boy hood in school and even how much his little sis loves and looks out for him and helps look for him after he goes missing). 

Drop them below...

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Way Way Back
2. Hunt For the Wilder People

and this is a series not a movie but: Reservation Dogs 
3. The Outsiders (1983)  
Stand By Me (1986)  
The Selfish Giant (2013)
4. Billy Elliot\
Young Hearts\
Mid80s
5. Stand By Me
6. Almost Famous!! Watch the extended edition, this movie is peak!
7. billy elliot

wonder (here the boy has a great relationship with his parents, but he receives bullying and struggles to fit in)

the karate kid (struggling to fit in, bullying)

a silent voice (it's an anime but I reccomend)

the perks of being a wallflower

a monster calls

close (2022)

mysterious skin
8. A Bronx Tale (1993)
9. This Is England - Great coming of age movie about finding family among other outcasts and misfits and the inner turmoil it causes when that family begins to fracture. It’s not specifically a “big brother-little brother” dynamic but there are older kids who act as big brother figures (for better and worse.)
10. The Way Way Back

## 4. Terminator 2: judgment day

**Score:** 15
**Body:** greatest sequel of all time, greatest sci-fi movie of all time, greatest action movie of all time, hell, greatest movie of all time.

i doubt there is a single film that can top terminator 2.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For sure in contention for greatest sequel of all time (a few candidates share that title imo, another one being Aliens, which is also Cameron), but greatest movie of all time is a stretch.

Still awesome though. Recently had the pleasure of seeing it in 70mm
2. How dare you blaspheme Gremlins 2
3. In my mind, the only real contender for all three (hell, four) was ALSO written and directed by the same guy.
  - Which one is that?
4. Aliens
  - Came here to say this. Talk about a sequel that ups the ante. T2 has a liquid metal Terminator that they never explained why it could go through the time travel machine because it doesn't have any living flesh. Aliens has space Marines and robotic loader suits and hip mounted large caliber machine guns and dropships and a queen alien in a hive.
    - And Newt trapped in the lab with that damn face sucker. 
5. I prefer Terminator movies when they lean more towards horror as with the first but it was a stroke of genius to take the second film into scifi/action territory

Truly one of the greatest sequels of all time
6. Its on all the time on sky movies and cinemax. I always plan to watch a bit but can't turn it off.

It is cinema at its most intense start to finish.
7. Amm ok?

## 5. Good movie to watch on a big screen??

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Recently got a TV thats so bit its like being at the cinema!! Super excited to watch something but Im not sure what… Needs to be something without too much sex/violence. Ideally something that’s visually exciting!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lawrence of Arabia
2. Interstellar without a doubt mate. Congrats on the TV!
3. Lord of the rings trilogy (extended edition), enjoy a weekend with that 
  - This is the right answer and sounds like a perfect weekend 
4. *Aladdin (1992)*

*Jumanji (1995)*

*Independence Day (1996)*
5. Space movies. Like Gravity, the Martian, Interstellar, or Project Hail Mary. 
6. Speed Racer (2008)
7. Dances With Wolves

A movie to this day I regret not seeing in a theater.
8. 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968)
9. Dune (both parts)

Interstellar 

Blade Runner

Lawrence of Arabia

Watch some of your old favourites too, on 4K Blu-ray. Top Gun looks great. Watch it just before watching Maverick!

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. In honor of Pride Month, let us all bask in the genius of The Birdcage

**Score:** 2029
**Body:** What a goddamn whirlwind for 1996! Nathan Lane wasn't officially out yet, Robin Williams was relatively fresh from Mrs. Doubtfire, and the amazing and gorgeous Hank Azaria was only known for his voice rolls. The star studded cast was beyond belief, and the up and comings were notable as well. A timeless recreation of a French stage comedy, brought in all practicality to film. I cherish it to this day, and likely all days. Bonsoir 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://i.redd.it/d4uyawffov5h1.gif
  - https://i.redd.it/59oxgz6hov5h1.gif
    - TWILA, TWILA, TWILA\~!

https://i.redd.it/uk6tk6clov5h1.gif
      - https://i.redd.it/8oapnuimov5h1.gif
        - https://i.redd.it/7vqr01onov5h1.gif
2. “I made you short?”
3. It's how I learned about Pirin tablets.
4. This movie is on my all-time favorites list. It's brilliant from start to finish, and what a pedigree! Mike Nichols knew exactly what he was doing and how to do it. And even amidst all the comedy, some of the most heartbreaking moments. Plus, how funny is it to see Calista Flockhart pre-Ally McBeal?!

Gene Hackman and Dianne Wiest are \*chef's kiss\* perfection.
  - The uncomfortable boredom Hackman caused when he is blathering on about purple mountains and golden fields of wheat is one of the best things I've ever watched. 
5. “I pierced the toast!”

“You’re taking your toothbrush to the cemetery? How Egyptian.”

Love this movie

## 2. A Movie you had high hopes for but it made you say :

**Score:** 1126

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Megalopolis - not sure why it's not a comment yet tbh, maybe few people actually saw it.
  - i saw it, my cinephile friend loved it, i was very open-minded about it, i wanted to love it, and i even thought some of the acting was good... but the movie was atrocious bullshit at the end of the day
  - The documentary they filmed while shooting Megalopolis is amazing. 

Its called Megadoc. People just listening to Francis Ford and looking around confused. Him old and losing control of the cast. Its amazing. 
2. Napoleon
  - It was like they tried to make a movie offensive to everything about Napoleon's story. 
3. Gladiator 2...... i knew it would never live up to the first one but my god i could barely make it through the movie 
  - You didn’t appreciate the sharks swimming through the Colosseum?😂
4. Joker 2
  - Spend $200 million just to purposefully anger all the fans of the original.
5. Wonder woman 84.  Not that I thought it would be great but recall we were deep in covid and it was presented as a big budget major movie.  After months of Netflix crap my hopes for a return to normal were so high.  It was horrific.  I couldn't understand why someone would actually make a movie so bad and not be ashamed of what they've done.

## 3. in Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, most of the new stars became pretty popular after this movie came out especially Austin Butler

**Score:** 1071
**Body:** Austin Butler, Maya Hawke, Mikey Madison, Julia Butters, Margaret Qualley, and Sydney Sweeney. This movie has a pack of new stars.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. TIL Sydney Sweeney was in Once Upon a Time in Hollywood 
  - I've finally got round to watching Handmaid's Tale.....that was a surprise.
    - That's how I felt when I rewatched Sharp Objects. Didn't realize it was her on the show.
2. I saw The Nice Guys (2016) recently and was pleasantly surprised to see Margaret Qualley in it. She is incredibly talented. 
  - One of the rare “nepo babies” I like more than their famous parent 
3. Victoria Pedretti missed out big time!!! 💔
4. Austin’s been acting since 2005. I remember watching him in the lead role of The Shannara Chronicles and telling my wife that he was going to be a big star.
5. I’ve always thought you’d have to be a complete moron to join a cult. Just a weak dumb little asshole but if Sydney Sweeney, Maya Hawke, Margot Qualley and Mikey Madison asked me to murder a bunch of people, fend off the government and drink the cool aide, it’s happening.
6. Don’t forget the blonde guy that beat up Bruce Lee
  - Ah yeah, Boomhauer's sleezy brother.

## 4. Can we officially declare 2026 as one of the best years for horror in a long time?

**Score:** 568
**Body:** and we’re not even halfway through the year.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. i think we’ve been on a good run since 2022 at least 
2. multiple hydrogen bombs vs one coughing baby here
  - Primate is very ungood
    - Faces of Death was awful. Those cops were terrible. 
3. Out of all these, I saw Send Help (is that really horror?), Over Your Dead Body (is that really horror?), Undertone, Obsession, Backrooms, and Hokum. Hokum was my favorite.


Yes, it's been a great year for horror. You didn't even have room for Exit 8 in your picture.
4. The Whistle was solid. Just saw Iron Lung, a tad long but I dug it

It’s been a great year

Edit: I watched Psycho Killer last night, high and having a couple beers. No expectations, nor knew anything about it. Decent late night movie, Devil shit, nothing groundbreaking but I had a good time
  - I’m going to throw Widows Bay in the mix here. It’s an Apple Mini Series but seriously some of best horror from anything in years I’m not kidding.
5. https://preview.redd.it/ihl53nyrys5h1.jpeg?width=352&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=c76dd47873448c52b87a2b51c27bf6ee23da73fb
6. This year has been unreal. As a die hard horror fan this is my Super Bowl. I hope some of these other studios give some of these young creative minds more chances. 
7. Not just movies but gaming as well has hit a huge spike in horror interest 

## 5. City of God this film still peak

**Score:** 481
**Body:** So I've rewatched this film again and still it's the best. This film will never replaced on my top 4 films. I like everything in this film.

Even though it was so violent and there are some gruesome scenes, I still liked it. I mean that was the point of the film right? It's to showcase what really does it mean in living in a violent environment. And that message was expressed well. For me this film like a war but instead of glorifying the war this was the opposite this is like anti-war or anti gang violence.

But I really want rocket and Angelica to be together in the end though, and bene just took the girl like bruhhh.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I need to watch this again. I remember it being fantastic and very relatable to spending most of my childhood on the streets.
2. It's a rite of passage for any international film lover. 

My fiancé is a Rio native. We visited her famil few years ago, the city of god favela was close to where we stayed. We were safe, but during the day I thought I could hear fireworks going off. My partner quickly corrected me, they were gunshots. 

That was 2024. It's a beautiful city with beautiful people. I will always want to go back there, just the extreme poverty and the will to survive causes people to do criminal things. You certainly need to be careful. 

A more recent film that also highlights the troubles of Brasil and especially its corruption is the secret agent. Fantastic movie, I recommend you watch it if you loved city of god.
3. Yep rewatched it a couple of months ago, still great. The scene with the two kids is horrifying 
  - When knockout Ned joined the war felt like watching wrestling. I was like ohhh shit here we go. 
4. HBO Max released a sequel tv show, called City of God: The Fight Rage On, in 2024 and a second season Is coming at some point. [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City\_of\_God:\_The\_Fight\_Rages\_On](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/City_of_God:_The_Fight_Rages_On)


  - Its the second tv show based on the movie I think.

There was a brazilian made city of god show around 15 years ago, with some of the cast from the movie.

It was called city of men.

5. This movie is just so good 
6. When I was a young teenager, this was my favourite film. I know I shouldn’t have been watching it but my uncle was an artist and he would give me all these cool films to watch that nobody I knew had even heard of. I thought it was one of the coolest films ever. I don’t really have a more intellectual description of my feelings towards it because I was probably 13 or 14.

Anyway, I watched it a few months ago. Still the coolest film I’ve ever seen hahaha
7. I think it says more about how crippling poverty can be rather than violence. The lack of opportunities in an area of the world that doesn’t get the funding it needs to help people escape poverty. But I agree on it being one of the greatest films ever.
8. Accurate observation.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The "long take" has stopped being a craft choice and started being a content marker

**Score:** 126
**Body:** Long takes used to mean something specific. Bazin's argument was about preserving spatial and temporal continuity, letting the viewer's eye choose what to look at, refusing the manipulative cut. Children of Men's long takes are doing actual Bazinian work because the continuity is the argument. The same scene cut conventionally would feel less true.

But somewhere in the last decade, the long take has decoupled from that argument. Now it functions more like a content marker, a signal of seriousness without the underlying formal logic. Sam Mendes's 1917 is the clearest example: the "single take" is a marketing concept, not a critical one. The film cuts constantly; we just can't see the cuts. The continuity is technical, not perceptual. It buys all the prestige of the long-take tradition without doing any of the work.

I see this in TV too, True Detective's six-minute oner became a watershed moment that producers learned to replicate without understanding what made it actually work in context.

The question isn't whether long takes are good or bad. It's whether the long take still earns the meaning we're attaching to it, or whether it's become aesthetic shorthand for prestige in the same way slow-motion did in the 2000s. I'd argue increasingly the latter.

Curious where you land on this.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would say there are two different things that are referred to as "long takes". Once is the "oner", where the camera just travels for a long stretch of time without cutting, usually following characters in and out of rooms, etc. These shots are more about creating a kinetic energy than anything else, and often land on close-ups or at least stick close to the actors. The focus here is often fairly shallow, kept on the actors, and there is usually a lot of active focus pulling work done as the camera moves. These are the ones we see a lot of in prestige TV and films like 1917 and Birdman.

The other type of long take, the one Bazin talks about, seen in things like Citizen Kane, is a lot gentler, often static, favours wider shots over close-ups, almost always employs deep focus, and as you say, has the distinct purpose of letting the viewer's eyes wander around the frame. In these shots, the dynamism often comes, not from camera movement, but from the actors moving through the space and traversing the planes while remaining in focus. There is a more observational and sensorial quality to these, as opposed to the more kinetic quality of the oners.

So really I'd say they might just be two different things, two different tools, that share the quality of not cutting .
2. I agree, I thought 'Adolescence' was a good programme and the opening scene of the police raid and arresting process worked really well as a one take. It showed the timeline really well particularly for the Dad it created a sense of not knowing what's going on. After the opening it felt like a gimmick to be discussed in the marketing of the film
  - I got hooked in episode one, which left me feeling immersed, and the episode in the school was also exciting, but the other two episodes seemed unnecessary and detracting more than adding.

The director Barantini also did Boiling Point, where the oner played a much different role, I feel. There it gave the film a very Safdie Bros vibe, which I also dug.

I think if the same director can use the same technique and get two very different effects, then there is still value in its modern usage. 
3. Your point about 1917 doesn't make a ton of sense. If we can't see the cuts then they have achieved perceptual continuity. And in doing so accomplished the essence of filmmaking which is to make all the technical heavy lifting appear seamless or imperceptible, ya know, "movie magic".

But furthermore it was effective. It was an artistic choice and it was done well, it made the movie feel immersive and intense.

Do you have other examples proving your point?

I can only think of examples that disprove your point. Not sure this "decoupling" you speak of has happened, in fact I'd say the it's gotten stronger and more effective as technology improved. 
  - I think sometimes there can be a fetish for difficulty in the creation of art; that something being challenging to create makes it more valuable. But as you said, if the technique being used is still effective and achieves an artistic purpose, being more difficult is a moot point. It’s like complaining about drum machines in music in 2026, or complaining about modern digital color grading that is seamlessly integrated.
4. Oners tend to draw attention to themselves now. At least in my perception. I don't know if it's just that we're savvy to the trend now so we judge them more cynically or if they're just not as intentional and well staged. 

Like, I just watched Hard Boiled again for the first time in a while and the famous oner during the hospital shootout didn't register at first because it's so seamless and the film so gripping. 

So many Old Hollywood films play out in a series of wides and masters, the camera observing space and character almost invisibly - composition changing elegantly without cuts for long periods of time. 

Spielberg is famous for his "mini oners" where the camera is so smooth & fluid you're not conscious of the composition changes in the moment.

Now so many films are blocked so basically and suddenly a oner happens purely for the director to show off.
5. I know you bring up Children of Men as a good example of long takes, but I've always felt that Roma was terrible for this. I despise the use of long takes in the movie, more or less for the reasons you describe here. It is just pretentious and shallow. I don't think this is exactly a new trend, more of a continuation of what has always been. Lumet put it best: *"Don't let the difficulty of actually achieving a shot make you think that the shot is good."* That's what a lot of these long takes feel like, it something to actually add to the film, but rather something to show off what you can do or to make the film seem more important than it actually is.  
 
One Cut of the Dead is a great example of when something being in a single take actually make the film work, as well as kind of poking fun at the kind of people who try and add prestige to a film just by having difficult to achieve shots.
6. There are TWO excellent ones in **SINNERS**, and I think a well-choreographed long shot like the complex six-minute tracking shot from TRUE DETECTIVE is a demonstration of craft. 

It’s a ballet, at their best, sequences like those. Fight scenes can get tiring but the JOHN WICK films also have some great long takes. 
  - There’s a great one in the Apple show, The Studio. Well, the entire episode was dedicated to the concept. They pulled it off brilliantly and with a decent amount of anxiety and humor.  Season 1, episode 2, titled The Oner. 
    - Yessss I saw that and it was hyper-cringe but also super-meta. 

The way Seth keeps fucking up the whole thing, as the extended joke….

I didn’t see the full show but I did catch the first few episodes:)

## 2. Thoughts on The Piano Teacher (2001) by Michael Haneke?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** This may seem weird to a lot of people, but I’ve never related more to a character in film than Erika. There’s something very uncomfortable about admitting that. Those shots where it focuses on her expressionless face for minutes at a time really capture that sense of being constantly self-aware, of monitoring yourself so closely that you forget what it means to act naturally. Nothing about her is allowed to move freely. It’s like her whole life is this horrific closed loop: the tighter the control, the more distorted the release; the more distorted the release, the more shame and fear, which then feeds back into even stricter control. Nothing ever balances out. It just escalates internally. There’s this expectation, especially in films that deal with repression or internal conflict, that something will break open in a meaningful way. The ending was so horrible and beautiful. It feels like she’s reduced everything, every feeling, every contradiction, every failed attempt at connection, down to a single cut to her chest because there is literally nowhere else for it to go. Not outward. Not into another person. Not even into words. There is no catharsis, and then she just continues. Ugh. It’s like her body is the only place anything can be released for her anymore because emotionally there’s nowhere else to go. Certainly the most disturbing but accurate portrait of repression I have ever seen.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I also relate to it very deeply. Isabelle Huppert gives maybe the best performance I've ever seen with Erika.

It's a character terrified of being known, always feeling scrutinized, and yet by looking at her and listening to her we do feel we know her. Huppert and the camera allow us in just enough. It's a delicate balance.

There's also an element of childishness to her that makes what she goes through so painful. She is just barely exploring the world, and so self-punishing as she does so.

Repression and anxiety has always felt like a spiral in my life, with gravity always pulling towards a single end. It is hard to watch that spiral play out quite viciously on screen.
2. Similar reaction to this film. There are only two films I've seen in my life that I can say I connected with so deeply that watching them was a physical experience, like a body blow. One was Andrew Haigh's Weekend, the other is The Piano Teacher. This film literally sent me to therapy. My connection to it set an alarm ringing in my head - you are not OK! That's when I went to therapy for the first time. And yes, admitting that feels uncomfortable, because Erika is such an extreme character. She's also a sexual pervert. I probably wouldn't talk about my connection to this film outside of Reddit. 

I won't start writing about the film because I've done so many times in the past, and nobody needs that particular wall again. To your point on the ending - I love your thought that her body is her only source of release. I guess this is the second time we see her cut herself for the release. I'd add the possibility that this is a positive ending for Erika. She's alive, and we know that as she leaves the concert hall she will survive. Her life will carry on as before. In some ways this is a triumph. At least she has this - her suffering. It's private and ineffectual, hidden from the world. But it's hers and she'll continue to bear it. To me she is like an iconic religious martyr. The self wounding at the end is framed that way by Haneke - Erika is central against a symmetrical background - the composition is very powerful, like a religious icon. And that's how Erika suffers, like one of the great martyrs. To me it's one of the all time great final scenes in cinema.

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (June 07, 2026)

**Score:** 14
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **No Hard Feelings (2023, Gene Stupnitsky)**

Not typically one for rom coms, I enjoyed this one! Jennifer Lawrence is great as Maddie, down on her luck and hired to date an awkward 19 year old about to leave for college. It’s about what you’d expect, but a fun watch. **6/10**

**Bedazzled (2000, Harold Ramis)**

This one didn’t age well at all! Brendan Fraser plays Elliot Richards, who is granted seven wishes by the Devil, Elizabeth Hurley. Relies heavily on outdated stereotypes and tropes, had me rolling my eyes throughout. **4/10**

**Eternity (2025, David Freyne)**

Mixed feelings about this one. I like media that explores the concept of an afterlife, but this one was essentially two men in a competition over who could win eternity with Elizabeth Olsen. It had some touching deeper moments but was pretty surface level for most of it. **6.5/10**
2. Been on a Michael Haneke run. Cache (Hidden) this week - the anxiety builds from scene one and never really lets up. Then Funny Games, which is a completely different kind of uncomfortable. Both leave you sitting with the discomfort long after. Anyone else going through a director's whole filmography lately?
3. Got hired to write a short film for an actor to intentionally display his acting chops, so I've been doing some research for the project. The short film I'm working on is an action/drama to be about seven minutes in length, yet tell a full, emotionally impactful story that might lead someone to say "Wow, I didn't realize you could fit so much story in such a short period of time." The films I've watched for research were:

*Warrior (2011)*

-First time watching this and loved it, though I couldn't help but root for a separate character than the film itself was rooting for.

*Run Lola Run (1998)*

-Awesome late 90s German crime thriller that never hits the brakes once it slams on the gas. It's been on my watchlist for years but never actually took the time to see it until now. Great execution and maintaining the film's intensity even when Lola isn't in motion, and she's almost always in motion.

*Ben-Hurr (1959)* 
 
-Another film that's been on my watchlist forever. Excellent cinematography and budding tension in action sequences. I also really enjoyed this experience due to the sheer scale of it -- in many scenes there are *loads* of people present, real actual people who aren't green screen projections, which is something I feel is lacking in a significant number of films today. Sure, it's likely due to budgets or story purposes, but it was refreshing to see a film that felt truly epic.

*The Raid: Redemption (2011)*

The Raid is an old favorite I've seen many-a-time, but it was the first time I watched it for business instead of pleasure. I was studying the overall pacing of the film and how it's an action movie that borrows elements from the horror/slasher genre once the police start being hunted by the criminals.

*28 Weeks Later (2007)*

-I rewatched this primarily for the intro, which showcases a peaceful safehouse being torn apart by a chaotic zombie horde in about seven minutes flat, perfect material for the film I'm working on. Even the rest of the movie holds up pretty well imo, it's no 28 Days Later but I feel it easily earns its place in the series.

Separately from my research for the short, I also watched both *Backrooms* and *Obsession* in theaters, and wow, what treats they were. 

While I felt *Backrooms* was lacking in following through on the mystery it presents, I felt the atmosphere and cinematography were both pretty strong and kept me engaged even when the creatures didn't. 

*Obsession* on the other hand might be my favorite horror film since 2023's *Late Night with the Devil* -- it was fantastic fun watching Inde Navarrette's performance as her character's possession continued to deepen over the runtime. Her performance carried the movie and made me love it a lot better than *Backrooms*.
  - >"Wow, I didn't realize you could fit so much story in such a short period of time."

With that goal in mind, I'd highly recommend checking out some of the short stories of Amy Hempel. I have never read a short story writer who can pack more emotional oomph in so few words. Obviously won't translate *directly* to screenwriting, but I find her choices in what to highlight vs. what to leave blank in the telling are excellent ways to think about writing character impact in general, across mediums.

[In the Cemetery Where Al Jolson is Buried](https://web.archive.org/web/20260520190620/http://fictionaut.com/stories/amy-hempel/in-the-cemetery-where-al-jolson-is-buried) is the easiest to find and the one most often recommended, but you can find a lot to love in any of her collections.
    - Excellent, thank you so very much for the rec. Tomorrow's my first day to sit at the library and generate some ideas so I'll start with the story you linked and see what others I can find. What you said about her choices regarding what to highlight vs. what to leave blank resonates with me because I was looking for inspiration in how I could richly characterize the protagonist in a short timeframe, so this sounds very relevant. 

Looking forward to diving in!
4. This week I've picked back up my project to watch and review every Kurosawa film in order, with *I Live in Fear* and *Throne of Blood* (which I graded a B- and A respectively). This was my first watch of *I Live in Fear* (one of the few Kurosawa movies I hadn't seen previously).

A few days ago I watched *The Backrooms*, which I felt was a great concept executed competently, in that the pieces fit together and basically worked, but it never felt like it had the ambition the concept warrants or the creative passion to really delve into new narrative spaces, instead using an interesting setting for very familiar story beats. Of the three big horror releases the last few weeks, it seems the most even-keel, lacking *Obsession*'s drive to push into uncomfortable territory and *Passenger*'s creative passion for shot and scene composition, but also not falling into the excesses of either of them. I think overall I liked it less than *Obsession* but more than *Passenger*. 

I'm trying to make time today for *Pressure*.

6. **Paprika (2006, dir. Satoshi Kon)**

Satoshi Kon has a reputation for being one of the great non-Ghibli anime directors, and oh boy does he strut his stuff in Paprika. It's a dense film, folding many story styles and genres into a trippy dream thriller a la Inception or Cell, although in the realm of animation it has so much more latitude than those works. I don't want to say that there's no finer symbolism, but if something like Mulholland Drive or The Sopranos uses dreams to shed light on a character's psyche, Paprika uses dreams to channel the most batshit imagery possible and package it into a story you couldn't hope to predict. Paprika is a wonderful, albeit dizzying film that still feels pertinent twenty years later. Dreams are the last human frontier untouched by commodification and pop culture, and dabbling with them will yield something that seemingly couldn't be imagined, consciously or unconsciously. God only knows how Kon did it.

**Ouija: Origin of Evil (2016, dir. Mike Flanagan)**

In 2014, Ouija came out and bored and infuriated critics everywhere. But it also made a bajillion dollars, and Jason Blum realized he had a hot new franchise on his hands. Fortunately, he entrusted the future of Ouija to Mike Flanagan, who took a generic 2010s ghost movie and turned it into a quality story about trauma, loss, and our inability to cope with death. Three marginal characters from the original film are turned into compelling protagonists, realized extremely well by Elizabeth Reaser, Annalise Basso, and Lulu Wilson. The concept of a family of con-artist mediums trying to take advantage of genuine supernatural talent is a fun one for a horror movie, and the emotional writing keeps the viewer engaged. In the first act, ordinary drama is offset by dark atmosphere and the looming sense of something waiting in the shadows, one of Flanagan's great skills.

A lot of the film's positive marks, though, fall by the wayside after the first act. It doesn't turn bad, but the plot ends up revolving around a possessed child, an already cliched concept that's given little new life here, especially since you know broadly how the film ends because you've seen Ouija. The second act is well-directed and well-acted but largely plodding. The third act does a lot to redeem the more mundane parts of the film, but it still feels more studio-y than the first act, where Flanagan's writing really shines. But still, Origin of Evil is no Ouija, and I have to give Flanagan credit for making an original, poignant movie out of something cynical and heartlessly dumb. He's set to release an Exorcist sequel next year, so hopefully, that film can follow similar beats to Origin of Evil while maintaining more consistent quality.

**An Even Break (1917, dir. Lambert Hillyer)**

One of the most degraded silent films I've ever seen. It's always a tragedy to watch one only for splotches to consume every frame until the end of a reel, or for the dreaded "MISSING REEL" intertitle to come up, but it's a little hard to feel too upset for An Even Break. From what I can gather from the tatters, it's a generic romantic drama from the 1910s with way too much plot. Do you need a forced love triangle *and* a corporate conspiracy to automate a factory if they don't ever meaningfully intersect? I don't think so. The most I can say about An Even Break is that I liked the core characters. I liked the contrast between them as children and them as adults, and I liked how convivial the two female leads were, how open they were to helping each other out in times of emotional and financial strife. But that was before the love triangle emerged. Perhaps in a better state, I would have appreciated the performances more, but as it is An Even Break has sadly succumbed to the forces of time.

**Animal Crackers (1930, dir. Victor Heerman)**

I'm peripherally a Marx Brothers fan. I've seen three or four features, but I can only reliably name Night at the Opera, and I haven't seen one in over ten years. But I have a very pleasant memory of Night at the Opera, and I went into Animal Crackers expecting something of that caliber. The Marxes did not deliver. As it turns out, Animal Crackers is their second feature, and an adaptation of a Broadway musical. This explains a lot; from a technical standpoint, the film is lacking, and the brothers aren't at their sharpest. The script is rambling and only loosely adheres to a plot, something wholly unrelated to what the Marxes are doing. The film's pace is excruciating, and is not helped at all by inconsistent comedy, itself handicapped by overlong bits. We don't need to hold on a static shot of a botched bridge game for five minutes to get the joke. By my memory, Night at the Opera did not have these issues. Hopefully my memory won't betray me, and the Marxes will fully improve within five years of making Animal Crackers.

**Whisper of the Heart (1995, dir. Yoshifumi Kondo)**

Delightful coming-of-age story. Hayao Miyazaki's script has great commentary on the artistic process, and this is realized beautifully by Kondo's gentle direction, bringing the audience into the world of a girl who's much too active and aware to be written off as a child but is not confident and autonomous enough to be mistaken for a young woman just yet. Shizuku fits the Ghibli heroine mold admirably, complex in her characterization but never just her flaws or idiosyncrasies. She's a fully fleshed-out person, weaving in and out of her social spheres deftly but also finding time to chase a cat just because it's there, or to appreciate the beauty of urban cityscapes that most people would not find aesthetically pleasing. Her most endearing and interesting trait is her creative spark. She loves to write and loves books and music, but for one reason or another, she has no confidence in her ideas or output, even though her friends consistently praise her and perfect strangers play accompaniment for her cover of Take Me Home, Country Roads. Her arc is not one of finding and realizing a dream, but one of learning how hard it is to commit to one and just how self-gratifying it can feel when something meaningful comes from that commitment. It's the one lesson every young artist needs to learn, and if you're emotionally honest and consistent enough, you will improve and make something that will really resonate with someone. I really like how direct this aspect of Whisper of the Heart is. It makes me wish I'd watched it as a teenager. If you are an artist - or not, really - this is a great watch. It's criminal that Kondo died before directing another feature. Miyazaki and Isao Takahata imagined that he would have been their heir to the studio's throne, and after seeing this film, it's easy to see why.

**Beyond the Black Rainbow (2010, dir. Panos Cosmatos)**

Stylistically ambitious. Cosmatos channels aspects of 2001, THX 1138, Lovecraftian existentialism, psychedelia, Lynchian horror, and '80s slasher movies into a slow, beautiful sci-fi horror film. The dialogue is minimal and often low, but it would be unfair to write off Beyond the Black Rainbow as something devoid of substance. Everything in the film is framed within the search for happiness and self-fulfillment; scientist (pseudoscientist?) Mercurio Arboria created an institution in the 1960s with the express purpose to heal people of their depression and anxiety, and almost two decades and a slew of purely evil experiments into psychotropic drugs and telekinetic research later, his facility is a cold warehouse where mutants, revenants, and psychic wild children are stored for no real end, just the blank, unfeeling curiosity of the staff. Characters like Elena, said psychic wild child, are miserable, but Arboria and main villain Nyle themselves find no personal satisfaction in their present circumstances. They're convinced there's something more to the universe than what can be found in the human soul, and people die as a result. It really does feel like a mid-tier Lovecraft story (minus the racism, of course): not a lot of real story, but the questions and style are interesting enough to prompt your own introspections. The ending is a little clunky, though. I don't mind the shift into a slasher movie, but I think that tying Nyle's rampage more directly to Elena's escape would have been a lot more satisfying. Still, it's worth watching. Directorial visions like Panos Cosmatos don't really grow on trees.

## 4. Is Guy Ritchie’s In the Grey an anti-film?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Watching *In the Grey,* I was genuinely taken aback by how it not only lacked but seemed completely disinterested in what you might consider to be the building blocks of action (or indeed any) filmmaking – from establishing and developing character and motivation, to creating tension within a scene or across the course of the film.

Was there a single scene where a main character’s dialogue was intended to reveal something meaningful about their personality? Did any character grow or change in any real way over the course of the film? Was there any inner turmoil at any point? Or a moment where we were actually led to fear for the safety of our three protagonists or doubt their ability to overcome the obstacles put in their path?

And to ask a less rhetorical question, was all of this a deliberate choice?

When even your big showdowns against the primary antagonists at the climax of the film are rushed through in barely a minute (with no jeopardy for our heroes whatsoever), when your main ‘goodies’ have zero conflict between or within themselves, and when there’s not even the slightest effort to wrap up the protagonists’ stories once >!they’ve recovered money for a faceless company that they didn’t even pretend we had a reason to care about!<, you have a level of slapdash incompetence that would be hard to rationalise in a first-time filmmaker, let alone an experienced hand like Ritchie.

It isn’t enough to say that it’s because he is making a lowest-common-denominator action film. Many other examples of that field at least go through the motions of trying to provide what is generally required of a movie – a token scene to establish some emotional investment, a thinly sketched character arc. These efforts may sometimes be painfully underwhelming, but there was at least an awareness that these things are usually needed.

I’m not sure *In the Grey* is even trying to do these things. So what is it trying to do? Be an attempt at a James Bond-style franchise of glamorous locations and untouchable action men/women? But the Bond films typically understood that to get away with your hero being an unchanging superhero, you needed some vulnerable characters around him, satisfying face-offs with colourful villains who presented a threat, and plots that might hurt innocents rather than money men. *In the Grey* doesn’t concern itself with any of this either.

Or is it a conscious escalation of the ‘Netflix movie’ formula? Where plot and character and anything that might take a second to digest or think through are dispensed with so completely that what remains is a series of frictionless, meaningless scenes with frictionless, meaningless dialogue, all of which actively avoids what is required to make a film satisfying. An anti-film, if you will.

That was my best guess as I emerged from the film today. Not a particularly shocking or sophisticated take, but I was surprised by how *In the Grey*’s ultra-careless anti-filmmaking got under my skin, and I was curious whether others agreed or felt I was overreacting (as I didn’t enjoy Ritchie’s brand of smug, swaggering, hypercapable protagonists swanning effortlessly through events in *The Gentlemen* or *The Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare* either).

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >And to ask a less rhetorical question, was all of this a deliberate choice?

Dude has been putting barely sketched out caricatures on screen since like 2015, so I really think it's a skill issue at this point. I don't know what happened for him to lose the writing chops as far as character goes, but... it's honestly quite tragic. Doesn't help though that for whatever reason the people he casts these days have no chemistry with each other whatsoever (or alternatively no desire to engage with the characters as written--I have seldom seen such lack-luster performances from anyone involved in Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare tbh)
  - > (or alternatively no desire to engage with the characters as written--I have seldom seen such lack-luster performances from anyone involved in Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare tbh)

You could tell me Ritchie told Cavill the cameras were off and to give his "not trying" performance for just about every scenes, and I would absolutely believe it.
    - No one involved in that movie tried at all, not even for a single moment at any stage in its development 
2. I think Ritchie has a bad case of firstdegreitis. Nowadays, he really only aspires to show us how Statham is badass or Cavill handsome. And like, that's cool on paper, but it's not really interesting.

Take The Gentlemen vs Snatch or Lock Stock... In his older gangster movies, we followed scrappy underdogs who had as much ridiculous moments as they had awesome ones. In The Gentlemen, we just follow the druglord protagonist being suave and manly and defeating the bad criminals while being good friend to the British aristocracy. It's a fun movie, but the spark is missing, the irony is absent. By being more direct, his movies become also, in a way, less authentic.
3. >And to ask a less rhetorical question, was all of this a deliberate choice?  
Or is it a conscious escalation of the ‘Netflix movie’ formula?

I think some of it is, but I don't get the sense he knows how to deliver on exactly what he's imagining.

Parts of Grey were very entertaining in isolation. I don't have a problem not knowing anything about the characters motivations or personality and just letting it be a technical heist movie. That will never be memorable but it may be entertaining, and that's sufficient.

And maybe you could have made it just that, but you can't ALSO stretch out into deeper side narratives because we aren't attached to the characters.

It's hard to say what he was going for since he seems to want both a cypher and a heist thriller and it's not clear if either would have worked on their own because the execution to partial on both.

## 5. Thoughts on Central Station (1998) by Walter Salles?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** For me, this was a film about God, or his absence, and how to act as if he might exist in this absence. The film circles him constantly, like people talking in a room where someone important is absent but still being listened for. I think the words painted on the back of that truck really sum it up: “Tudo é força, só Deus é poder” (“Everything is strength, only God is power”) The boy’s absent father who he is searching for the entire film being named Jesus is also very on the nose.
